metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `author_id` (or `identity_id`) to the approved five-field ledger schema in Section 3 so Section 4's `verify_authorship_at_commit` call has a source for the required identity parameter.
findings.finding: The new ledger schema `{event_uuid, content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position}` contains no `author_id` field, yet Section 4 (`_ledger_events` + `_verify_signed`) specifies that ledger verification calls `verify_authorship_at_commit(envelope, author_id, commit_sha, position, repo_root=...)` — `author_id` is a required parameter of that confirmed function signature (authorship.py:163) but has no home in the new record. An executor building Section 4 cannot supply this argument from the ledger alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the ledger record to six fields: `{event_uuid, content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position, author_id}` — `author_id` records the signing identity at compaction time, matching the existing `_build_authorship_ledger` pattern and allowing Section 4 to call `verify_authorship_at_commit(envelope, author_id, commit_sha, position, ...)`; update AC2 to assert the ledger record has exactly those six keys.

findings.finding: The new ledger schema (`{event_uuid, content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position}`) has NO `author_id` field, yet the plan's Section 4 says ledger verification is 'self-contained' — but `_verify_signed` today short-circuits via `_is_identity_author(ev.author_id, tracker)`. If ledger `_ScopedEvent` entries populate `author_id=None` (since it's not in the new schema), `_verify_signed` will always return `UNKNOWN_AUTHOR` for ledger entries, defeating the self-contained verification claim. The plan does not address how `author_id` is supplied (or bypassed) for the ledger path.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) store the `identity_id` in the ledger record (a sixth field) so the existing `_is_identity_author` gate works, or (b) skip the `_is_identity_author` check for ledger entries and verify directly against the single-key trust root built from `signer_pubkey`, routing through a separate `_verify_ledger_entry` helper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or extend AC4 to include a ledger-path test: sign events with a forged key, compact (horizon=0, threshold=1), delete raw event files, then assert `verify-authorship --all` returns `bad-signature` from the ledger alone (exercising the null-`signer_pubkey` → bad-signature branch in `_ledger_events`).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the null-`signer_pubkey` ledger path end-to-end: Section 3 specifies that a forged/foreign signature should still produce a ledger entry with `signer_pubkey: null`, and Section 4 says that entry is classified `bad-signature` directly. But AC4 only tests `bad-signature` for the LIVE-event (pre-compaction) case; no AC verifies the round-trip 'forge → compact → verify-from-ledger → bad-signature' path through the null-signer_pubkey branch.
findings.location: AC4 (`bad-signature` test); Section 3 single-None-policy; Section 4 `_ledger_events` null handling
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4: 'A round-trip test also compacts a forged event (signer_pubkey: null in ledger), retires the raw file, and asserts verify-authorship --all emits bad-signature from the ledger record alone.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) that drives: compact an event bearing a forged author_sig (verifies against no key the identity holds) with horizon=0/threshold=1, then run `verify-authorship --all` from the ledger alone and assert it emits `bad-signature` — covering the null-signer_pubkey ledger path end-to-end.
findings.finding: The null-signer_pubkey ledger path — where identify_signer returns None (forged/foreign signature), compact records signer_pubkey:null, and post-compaction verify-authorship --all must emit bad-signature from the ledger entry alone — is a new behavior in _ledger_events rewrite with no explicit acceptance criterion. AC4 covers bad-signature for live events only; none of the ACs drive compact → null-signer_pubkey ledger entry → verify-authorship --all → bad-signature end-to-end.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4: 'A test also compacts a forged-signature event (horizon=0, threshold=1) and asserts verify-authorship --all returns bad-signature from the ledger entry (signer_pubkey:null), confirming the ledger-only null-key path is exercised independently of the live-event path.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that identify_signer returning None causes the ledger to 'omit / record unsigned' the signer_pubkey field, but no AC or test exercises this failure/empty path — only the happy path (signer_pubkey equals the actual signing key) is tested in AC2. This is a new user-facing ledger field with a specified but untested edge-case behavior.

The 'What' section states that ledger verification emits 'the same four-way verdict' for all cases including non-happy paths (key_not_valid_at_era, bad-signature, unknown-author), but no AC tests any non-verified verdict emitted from the ledger path — AC2 only tests `verified` from ledger, and AC3/AC4 describe live-event paths without specifying ledger coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit fallback for `resolve_event_commit` failure at compaction time (e.g. 'record `commit_sha: null`; Section 4 ledger verification treats a null commit_sha as `bad-signature`') so the executor has a directed default.
findings.finding: When `resolve_event_commit` fails at COMPACTION time (Section 3, `_build_authorship_ledger`), the plan specifies no fallback behavior. It explicitly handles the live-event failure case ('the fail-closed verify yields non-verified') but is silent for the compact path. An executor must choose between: aborting the compaction, recording `commit_sha: null`, or skipping the ledger entry — each has different downstream consequences for Section 4 ledger verification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 3: 'If `resolve_event_commit` fails at compaction time, record `commit_sha: null` in the position object. Section 4 ledger verification: a null `commit_sha` is treated identically to a null `signer_pubkey` — classified `bad-signature` directly without attempting era verification.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `verify_authorship_any_key` returns `registry.Verdict` (not a raw bool) and that callers check `.verified`, or change the helper's return type to `bool` explicitly — to prevent the 'truthy object' misread that would mis-classify forged signatures as `key_not_valid_at_era`.
findings.finding: The plan describes `verify_authorship_any_key` as running 'ONE `registry.verify` (boolean)', but `registry.verify` returns a `Verdict` object (not a raw boolean); callers must check `.verified`. While the integration is feasible, the 'boolean' description may mislead the implementer into writing `if registry.verify(...):` (which is always truthy as an object) instead of `if registry.verify(...).verified:`.
findings.location: Section 2, Merge-gate rewire — four-way classification: 'key_not_valid_at_era' bullet, 'runs ONE registry.verify (boolean)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's description to: 'runs ONE `registry.verify(...)` call and returns `verdict.verified` (bool)' — OR define `verify_authorship_any_key -> bool` explicitly in the spec so the implementer knows the return type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan explicitly requires updating the hardcoded print format string (line 328-331) in `verify_authorship.py::cli` to include `key_not_valid_at_era` in the printed summary, not just the `counts` dict initialization.
findings.finding: The CLI summary print in `verify_authorship.py` (line 328-331) is a HARDCODED FORMAT STRING, not driven by the `counts` dict. The plan says 'extend the CLI counts dict with the new key so it is tallied and printed,' but adding `KEY_NOT_VALID_AT_ERA` to the `counts` dict initialization (line 317) alone will not cause the new verdict to appear in the printed summary line — the literal format string at line 328-331 also must be updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `key_not_valid_at_era` to the format string, e.g.: `f"{counts[VERIFIED]} verified, {counts[UNSIGNED]} unsigned, {counts[UNKNOWN_AUTHOR]} unknown-author, {counts[BAD_SIGNATURE]} bad-signature, {counts.get(KEY_NOT_VALID_AT_ERA, 0)} key_not_valid_at_era"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in Section 2 that the hardcoded CLI summary format string in `verify_authorship.py` must be updated to include `key_not_valid_at_era` in the printed output, not only in the `counts` dict initializer.
findings.finding: The CLI summary print in `verify_authorship.py` (lines 327–332, confirmed by reading the file) is a hardcoded format string: `f'{counts[VERIFIED]} verified, {counts[UNSIGNED]} unsigned, {counts[UNKNOWN_AUTHOR]} unknown-author, {counts[BAD_SIGNATURE]} bad-signature'`. The plan says 'extend the CLI counts dict (currently hardcoded {VERIFIED, UNSIGNED, UNKNOWN_AUTHOR, BAD_SIGNATURE}) with the new key so it is tallied and printed' but does not mention updating the hardcoded format string. Adding `KEY_NOT_VALID_AT_ERA` to `counts` alone leaves it silently absent from the printed summary line.
findings.location: Section 2 ('extend the CLI counts dict'), `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` lines 317, 327–332
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 2: 'Update the hardcoded summary print to include the new verdict: e.g. drive the summary from the counts dict dynamically (`", ".join(f"{v} {k}" for k, v in counts.items())`) rather than a hardcoded format string, so future verdicts are automatically included.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which verdict (`bad-signature` is the natural fail-closed choice) is emitted when `resolve_event_commit` fails for a LIVE event, and add a test assertion confirming that specific verdict is produced in that error path.
findings.finding: When `resolve_event_commit` fails for a LIVE event, the plan says the verification is 'fail-closed' and the event is 'reported like any other non-verified event', but it does not specify WHICH of the three non-verified verdicts (`bad-signature`, `unknown-author`, or `key_not_valid_at_era`) is emitted. This ambiguity affects CLI users trying to diagnose why verification failed: `bad-signature` implies tampering/forgery, `unknown-author` implies an identity problem, and `key_not_valid_at_era` implies key-rotation timing — each suggests a different remediation.
findings.location: Section 2 — Merge-gate rewire, paragraph: 'When resolve_event_commit fails for a LIVE event (unresolvable commit), the fail-closed verify yields non-`verified` and the event is reported like any other non-verified event.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the merge-gate rewire section, add: 'When `resolve_event_commit` raises or returns None, emit `bad-signature` (commit resolution is treated as a prerequisite to signature verification; inability to establish the commit context means the signature cannot be considered valid at any era).' Add a corresponding AC assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_state.py`'s `authorship` summary (and `_replay.py`'s tally loop) requires any changes for this story, and if so add `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` and `src/rebar/reducer/_replay.py` to `file_impact`.
findings.finding: The plan mentions `_state.py`'s authorship summary as in-scope ('the write-seam signing, the merge-gate, and the ledger all live in THIS commit … and `_state.py`'s authorship summary') but `_state.py` is absent from `file_impact`. The `authorship` field in `_state.py` is `{signed, unsigned}` — if the four-way verdict introduces any new authorship-presence counting categories or if `authorship` in compiled_state needs extension, `_state.py` and `reducer/_replay.py` are unenumerated edit targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly document `verify_authorship_any_key`'s contract in `authorship.py`'s spec: state that the caller reads `.verified` (not `.verdict`) as the boolean, and spell out the compound rule (`.verified == True AND verify_authorship_at_commit(...).verified == False ⇒ key_not_valid_at_era`) in the helper's docstring or in a co-located decision table in Section 2.
findings.finding: The decision rule that maps the `verify_authorship_any_key` result to the `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict ('valid-any-key AND NOT valid-at-commit ⇒ key_not_valid_at_era') is specified only as inline plan prose in Section 2, not co-located with the helper's own specification in `authorship.py`'s file-impact entry. `verify_authorship_any_key` returns `registry.Verdict` (the plan specifies `-> registry.Verdict`) but the caller in `verify_authorship.py` must know to read `.verified` for the boolean and apply the compound condition — which field to read and how to combine it with the `verify_authorship_at_commit` result is never made explicit in the helper's contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `verify_authorship_any_key`'s behavior when the identity's full keyring (including revoked keys) is empty: document whether this case returns a non-verified Verdict that the caller treats as `bad-signature` or falls through to the `unknown-author` classification in `_verify_signed`.
findings.finding: `verify_authorship_any_key` and `identify_signer` are new helpers the plan adds to `authorship.py`, but neither exists in the current `authorship.py` (confirmed by search). The plan specifies their signatures and high-level behavior but not their fallback for the sub-step where `_keyring_for` returns an empty list (keyless or unknown identity): `verify_authorship_any_key` with zero keys would call `registry.verify` against an empty trust root — the outcome (verified=False with verdict 'unknown_principal' per `verify_authorship_at_commit` pattern, or an sshsig error) is unspecified and differs from the `bad-signature` classification the caller needs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the inner `position` string subfield to a distinct name (e.g. `event_position` or `position_key`) throughout Sections 3, 4, and AC2 to eliminate the `position.position` self-reference.
findings.finding: The ledger record field `position` is a JSON object whose only string subfield is ALSO named `position` — producing `position.position` references in Section 4. This overloads the same identifier for both the outer struct and the inner `{ts}-{uuid}` string that `verify_authorship_at_commit` separately consumes as a positional argument, creating a likely implementer confusion point between the struct and the string.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ledger record shape to `{event_uuid, content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, commit_sha, event_position}` (flat) or keep the nested object but rename the inner string to `event_position`: `position: {commit_sha, event_position: "<{ts}-{uuid}>"}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-subprocess timeout (e.g., `timeout=N` seconds) for `identify_signer`'s `ssh-keygen` invocations and define the error behaviour (treat as non-match, log warning, continue iteration) so compaction cannot hang indefinitely.
findings.finding: `identify_signer` runs an unbounded loop of `ssh-keygen -Y verify` subprocesses (one per key in the identity's full keyring including revoked keys) with no timeout specified. A hung subprocess (e.g., a stale NFS-backed key file or a misconfigured ssh-keygen) would block compaction indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `subprocess.run(..., timeout=10)` (or configurable) around each ssh-keygen call in `identify_signer`; catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired`, log a warning, and treat the result as a non-match for that key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the Statement-parse + subject-binding sub-steps from `verify_event_authorship` into a standalone helper (e.g. `authorship.parse_authorship_statement_binding(envelope, event) -> Verdict | None`) so `_verify_signed` can call it before dispatching to `verify_authorship_at_commit`, rather than inlining the same parse logic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies content-binding logic inline in `verify_authorship.py::_verify_signed` (parse the DSSE payload as an in-toto Statement, check `subject[0].name == ev['uuid']` AND `subject[0].digest.sha256 == authorship.authorship_content_hash(ev)`) that is already implemented in `authorship.verify_event_authorship`. The only reason it can't be called directly is that `verify_event_authorship` delegates to `verify_authorship` (current keys), not `verify_authorship_at_commit`. The Statement-parse and subject-binding sub-steps are duplicated rather than extracted.
findings.location: Section 2 — Merge-gate rewire (`verify_authorship.py`), LIVE-event content binding sub-bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add `authorship.check_statement_binding(envelope: dsse.Envelope, event: dict) -> registry.Verdict | None` that returns a non-verified Verdict on parse/shape failure or subject mismatch, else None (pass-through). Both `verify_event_authorship` and the new `_verify_signed` call this helper, then each dispatches to its own verify variant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the plan that `compact.py::_build_authorship_ledger`'s docstring (referencing `epoch_for_position`) must be updated when the function body is rewritten.
findings.finding: `epoch_for_position` (called at `compact.py:97`) and `verify_authorship_at_epoch` (called at `verify_authorship.py:135`) do not exist in `authorship.py` — they were apparently removed by TS2 — yet the current live code in commit da27c5f30 still calls them. The plan correctly targets both callers for rewrite, but the `file_impact` section does NOT mention `compact.py`'s docstring (line 77) which still refers to `epoch_for_position` by name. This is a minor unenumerated dead-reference cleanup.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the docstring in `_build_authorship_ledger` to reference the new schema fields (`content_hash`, `signer_pubkey`, `position`) rather than the removed `epoch_for_position` call.

findings.finding: `_state.py` is named in 'Stack scoping' as part of commit da27c5f30 (3183) being reworked by this story ('_seam.py`'s write-time signing, `verify_authorship.py`, `compact.py`'s ledger, and `_state.py`'s authorship summary'), yet it is absent from `file_impact`. The `_state.py` already seeds `authorship: {signed:0, unsigned:0}` correctly. If the plan intends a change there it must be listed; if no change is needed, the Stack Scoping claim is misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: If the authorship summary shape (`{signed, unsigned}`) needs no new fields for the 5-verdict model, drop `_state.py` from the Stack Scoping sentence. If the `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict should be tracked in the reducer presence summary (it probably shouldn't — the plan explicitly says presence only), state that explicitly.

findings.finding: The Stack Scoping preamble asserts `create_placeholder` already exists as a present-tense alias over `ensure_identity_for` in commit 6 ('ensure_identity_for (aliased by `create_placeholder`) lives in commit 6'), but `create_placeholder` is entirely absent from the codebase — no definition, no import, no export anywhere.
findings.location: Stack scoping section, paragraph 1: 'ensure_identity_for (aliased by `create_placeholder`) lives in commit 6'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'ensure_identity_for (aliased by `create_placeholder`) lives in commit 6' to 'ensure_identity_for lives in commit 6; `create_placeholder` is a NEW alias added by this story (Section 6)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out / rollback plan (e.g. 'compact to a fresh snapshot before revert; old-format snapshots contain no ledger entries so a post-revert snapshot is clean') or an expand-contract note explaining why revert is safe (e.g. the store is always rebuilt from scratch).
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the ledger wire-format change and write-seam format change but provides NO explicit rollback or back-out path. After landing, in-toto-format `author_sig` fields and the new `{content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position}` ledger rows would be unreadable by any reverted code. The migration note addresses only the forward direction ('no in-place snapshot migration to write') — acknowledgement alone does not satisfy T4's requirement for a reversibility mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to the Migration note: 'To revert after landing: (1) any snapshot containing new-format ledger rows must be discarded and rebuilt from raw events with the reverted code; (2) events with DSSE author_sig fields written by the new seam can be re-signed or treated as unsigned by the reverted merge-gate.' If the store is always cold-rebuilt (no persistent snapshots survive a code revert), state that explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before this story lands, capture the four-way verdict trust model, the `signer_pubkey: null` single-none-policy, and the `identify_signer` all-keys-including-revoked rationale in at least a CLAUDE.md section or a lightweight design-doc stub (TS4 can expand this into a full ADR, but the core rationale should be durable at merge time).
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — the four-way verdict trust model (valid-any-key AND NOT valid-at-commit ⇒ `key_not_valid_at_era`), the `signer_pubkey: null` single-none-policy (record forged events rather than omit), and the `identify_signer` all-keys-including-revoked iteration pattern — but explicitly defers ALL ADR/doc capture to TS4. These are security-relevant, non-obvious design choices; if TS4 slips or is resequenced, no durable record exists in the codebase, leaving future maintainers (and code reviewers of this story) without a reference for WHY these choices were made.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out: … docs/ADR (TS4)'); Section 2 (four-way classification); Section 3 (Single None-policy); Section 4 (ledger verification logic)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Authorship Verification Architecture` section to `CLAUDE.md` (or a `docs/authorship-verification.md` stub) covering: (1) the four-way verdict definitions and their decision tree, (2) why `identify_signer` iterates revoked keys and what `signer_pubkey: null` means, (3) the display grouping (`verified` / `unverified` / `unsigned`). TS4 can promote this to a full ADR; the goal here is a durable reference at merge time, not a polished document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit log warnings (at WARNING level or above) in `compact.py` when `identify_signer` returns `None` for a signed event (potential forgery) and when `resolve_event_commit` fails during compaction, so operators can observe these conditions without re-running verify-authorship.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no logging, metric, or alert for two new observability-critical conditions introduced at compaction time: (a) a signed event whose `identify_signer` returns `None` (possible forgery silently recorded with `signer_pubkey: null`), and (b) a `resolve_event_commit` failure (unresolvable commit recorded or skipped). Operators have no signal that forged events or commit-resolution failures occurred during compaction.
findings.location: Section 3 (Ledger — compact.py), 'Single None-policy'; Section 2 merge-gate, 'resolve_event_commit fails'; src/rebar/_commands/compact.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and implement) that `_ledger_events` treats a missing or empty `authorship_ledger` section in the snapshot as an empty list (zero ledger events), with no error raised.
findings.finding: `_ledger_events` is being rewritten (Section 4) to read the new schema fields, but the plan does not specify how it handles a snapshot that contains no `authorship_ledger` section at all (pre-compaction or pre-feature snapshot). Without an explicit contract, a missing ledger could raise a KeyError or return an incorrect result.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_ledger_events`, guard with `snapshot.get('authorship_ledger', [])` before iterating, and document this contract in the function's docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether AC6's requirement for `rebar.create_placeholder` is externally contractual (e.g. promised in an external spec or consumer code); if it is purely an internal naming preference, consider satisfying AC6 by documenting `rebar.ensure_identity_for` as the canonical `create_placeholder` equivalent rather than adding a second exported symbol.
findings.finding: `create_placeholder` is proposed as a thin alias over the already-exported `ensure_identity_for` with an identical signature. It has zero existing call-sites in the codebase, and `ensure_identity_for` is already in `rebar/__init__.__all__` and importable as `rebar.ensure_identity_for`. The alias adds a second public name for the same function, proliferating the API surface without adding new behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `verify_authorship_any_key` and `identify_signer` should compose with the existing `verify_event_authorship` helper (which already does Statement-parse + subject-binding + key-verify), or document why a separate implementation is required.
findings.finding: `verify_authorship_any_key` and `identify_signer` are described as NEW helpers to be added to `authorship.py`, but `authorship.py` already exports `verify_event_authorship` which performs in-toto Statement + content-binding + current-key verify in one call. The plan does not acknowledge this pre-existing helper or explain why it cannot be reused/extended for the `verify_authorship_any_key` (all-keys) path, leaving ambiguity about whether the new helper should compose with `verify_event_authorship` or duplicate its Statement-parsing logic.
findings.suggested_fix: `verify_authorship_any_key` could be specified as: call `verify_event_authorship` using a trust root built from ALL keyring keys (including revoked), reusing the Statement-parse path, rather than re-implementing it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that TS2 incremented `_REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION` (currently 6 in `reducer/_cache.py`); if not, this story must bump it to ensure stale reductions with the old `{author_id, author_sig, epoch}` ledger schema are not served to the new `_ledger_events` reader.
findings.finding: The plan states 'the reducer cache version bump from TS2 already invalidates stale reductions' as a premise for omitting a migration note, but the current `_REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION = 6` in `reducer/_cache.py` cannot be confirmed to have been bumped by TS2 (vs. being the pre-TS2 value). If TS2 did NOT bump the cache version, old snapshots with the `{event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch}` ledger schema would be served from cache to TS3's new `_ledger_events` reader, which expects `{content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position}` — causing silent misclassification.
findings.location: Migration note section — 'the reducer cache version bump from TS2 already invalidates stale reductions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale to the `authorship.py` section explaining why `verify_authorship_any_key` and `identify_signer` are two separate helpers rather than a unified classifier — e.g., note that they serve different callers (gate classification vs. ledger pre-computation), have different return types (`Verdict` vs. `str | None`), and that the ledger build happens once at compaction while the gate path is per-verification, making the separation a clean interface boundary despite the two traversals.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new `authorship.py` helpers — `verify_authorship_any_key` (one-shot full-keyring verify for the gate's `key_not_valid_at_era` path) and `identify_signer` (per-key iteration for ledger `signer_pubkey`) — without stating a positive rationale for why these are two separate functions rather than a single unified helper (e.g., `classify_key_validity(envelope, identity_id, *, repo_root) -> (verified_any: bool, matching_key: str | None)`). The plan describes *what* each does but not *why* the split is better than a combined helper that would avoid the double iteration through the full keyring.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: '`verify_authorship_any_key` and `identify_signer` are intentionally separate: the gate needs a `Verdict` (boolean pass/fail), the ledger builder needs the actual matching key string for durable storage; combining them would couple the gate's verification contract to the ledger's storage concern. The double traversal is bounded by the number of historical keys per identity (typically ≤ 5) and is a compile-time cost (once at compaction), not a hot-path cost.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `signer_pubkey` can be captured at sign time (from `signing_key + '.pub'` or `ssh-keygen -l`) and stamped onto the event/ledger record directly in `_apply_authorship`, eliminating the need for the post-hoc `identify_signer` N-subprocess iteration at compaction time; document the justification if the N-call approach is retained.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `identify_signer` — a helper that iterates ALL keyring public keys one-by-one via N separate `registry.verify` (ssh-keygen subprocess) calls at compaction time — without considering the simpler sign-time alternative: at the moment of signing (`_apply_authorship` / `sign_event_authorship`), the signer already has the exact private key path and could derive or record the corresponding public key fingerprint directly into the ledger record (e.g. via `ssh-keygen -l -f <pubkey>` or by reading the `.pub` file alongside `signing_key`). This would make `identify_signer` unnecessary and would reduce compaction cost from O(K × L × subprocess) to zero. The plan asserts the N-call approach is needed because "ssh-keygen -Y verify returns only pass/fail" but does not justify why the signer's public key cannot be captured at write time.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_apply_authorship`, after signing, read the public key from `<signing_key>.pub` (the OpenSSH convention: private key path + '.pub') and include it as a field in the authored event or pass it to `_build_authorship_ledger` so the ledger entry can be populated directly without per-key subprocess iteration. This is zero extra subprocesses and avoids the retroactive identification problem for revoked keys entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema state assertion to the migration note: specify that rolling back TS3 code requires either (a) purging snapshot caches (triggering a rebuild from raw events) or (b) confirming that the TS2 cache-version key causes new-format ledgers to be skipped gracefully by old readers — and document which path is taken.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'no in-place snapshot migration to write' and 'lands atomically,' but never specifies the schema STATE AFTER a partial rollback of TS3. On-disk snapshot files written after landing contain the new ledger format ({event_uuid, content_hash, signature, signer_pubkey, position}); if TS3 code is reverted without also purging cached snapshots, the reverted reader (expecting the old {event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch} schema) would encounter unreadable ledger entries. The plan neither commits to a snapshot-purge step on rollback nor asserts that the new cache-version key (bumped in TS2) makes old readers skip new-format ledgers gracefully.
findings.location: Migration note section ('Migration note (unlanded + atomic)') and Section 4 ('Ledger verification')
findings.suggested_fix: In the migration note, add: 'Rollback path: if TS3 is reverted, operators must delete on-disk snapshot files containing new-format ledger entries (or confirm the TS2 cache-version bump causes new-format snapshots to be discarded and rebuilt). The reverted verify_authorship.py must never silently read a new-format ledger as old-format. Recommended: the TS2 cache-version key should be incremented again on rollback to force a clean rebuild, or the ledger reader should emit a clear version-mismatch error rather than silently misreading fields.'

findings.finding: The behavior when `identify_signer` returns `None` for an event that IS present in the ledger (i.e., a 'signed' event that no current keyring key verifies) is contradictory: the plan says 'None ⇒ omit / record unsigned' but the ledger schema specifies exactly five fields including `signer_pubkey`, and the plan also says ledger entries are only written 'per signed folded event' — leaving it unclear whether the record is written with a null `signer_pubkey`, omitted entirely, or treated as bad-signature at compaction time.
findings.location: Section 3 (Ledger) — `signer_pubkey` description; AC2 'asserts the ledger record has exactly those five keys'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'None ⇒ omit / record unsigned' with: 'None ⇒ write the record with signer_pubkey: null; the ledger-verification step will classify this entry as bad-signature. The five-key schema is always emitted (signer_pubkey is nullable).' Update AC2 to note signer_pubkey is non-null only for verifiable signatures.

findings.finding: Section 3 says `identify_signer` returns `None => omit / record unsigned` but these two outcomes directly contradict each other (omit = no ledger entry; record unsigned = entry with null signer_pubkey), and Section 4 assumes `signer_pubkey` is present for self-contained verification while AC2 asserts the ledger records only 'signed folded events' with `signer_pubkey` equaling the signing key. An implementer faces two incompatible code paths.
findings.location: Section 3 (Ledger) — 'None ⇒ omit / record unsigned'; Section 4 (Ledger verification); AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'None ⇒ omit / record unsigned' with 'None ⇒ omit the ledger entry (do not write a record for events where identify_signer finds no matching key)'; update Section 4 to note that ledger entries are guaranteed to carry a valid signer_pubkey.

findings.finding: `identify_signer` returning `None` is described with two mutually exclusive behaviors in the same sentence: 'None ⇒ omit / record unsigned'. Omit means no ledger entry is written; record unsigned means a ledger entry is written with `signer_pubkey: null`. These are contradictory and leave implementors to choose arbitrarily, breaking the 'exactly five keys' AC2 assertion and the ledger verification path in Section 4 (which must handle or skip entries without signer_pubkey).

The four-way verdict decision protocol for ledger entries (Section 4) is structurally incompatible with the four-way protocol defined for live events (Section 2). Section 4 says ledger verification uses 'a ONE-key trust root built from `signer_pubkey`' and 'decide era from `position.commit_sha` against the SURVIVING snapshot keyring via verify_authorship_at_commit'. But `verify_authorship_any_key` (the gate for `key_not_valid_at_era`) operates against ALL keyring public keys — not against `signer_pubkey`. The plan says 'emitting the same four-way verdict' without specifying how `key_not_valid_at_era` is reached from a single-key trust root: a single-key trust-root verify that fails cannot distinguish 'wrong era' from 'forged' without a secondary call, which the ledger path description omits.
findings.location: Section 4 (Ledger verification, `verify_authorship.py::_ledger_events` + `_verify_signed`) vs Section 2 four-way classification

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
